this is an opinion that i have echoed many times previously. 

start with ONLY 1 account and work towards a payout. 

once you get the payout, leverage it for more accounts with the SAME FIRM.

result.message:
there are 2 outcomes when you take a trade: you either WIN or LOSE. 

you can't always win every trade. it's impossible to win 100% of your trades (with a large enough, statistically significant sample size). so, you are going to have losses. 

there's 2 ways to take a loss: a controlled loss, or an uncontrolled loss. controlled losses have to be factored into your system. as I said before, you will lose. so you have to factor in "losing" as a part in your profitable system. 

now, the bad stuff. uncontrolled losses are the ones that make a big dent in your "equity curve."  start tilting/overtrading/forcing trades, and you are in the territory for an uncontrolled loss. uncontrolled losses are the ones that will blow up accounts, set you back days/weeks of progress, and also possibly trigger the "failure funnel". 

I get asked this a lot as well. 
"How are you able to hold to TP?"
"How are you able to not overtrade or be selective?"

I do not remember what trades I took last week. I will not remember today's trades tomorrow. it doesn't matter to me. the outcome does not matter to me. all that matters to me is whatever i'm making daily progress and controlling my losses. do not look too much into the daily variance, unless the daily variance is what is killing you. and that ties back to falling into the habit of taking "uncontrolled losses".
result.title: on trade outcomes.

result.message:
i've seen friends and traders come and go over the years. seen traders multiply their accounts, only to give it all back. seen many times traders run up their account 5x/10x/20x even, then lose it all months later. what comes easy often goes easy. 

from the circles I was in 4-5 years ago, only a small, very small handful are still here and winning. and there's no coincidence they’re also the ones winning the most now. it takes years upon years of this to get to this level. 

you gotta have this almost delusional belief in yourself. never rely on others. as dark as this sounds, there is no one that cares about your success more than yourself. 

this game WILL break you. 

-you will go thru drawdown. more than you ever thought you would. 
-your patience will be tested. more than what you see online. 
-you will be tempted to blow it all on one bad decision. very few are immune to FOMO. 

no one can sit in on that pain for you. no one will manage your risk for you. you either learn it or don't. at the end of the day, it's a choice. YOUR choice. 

you either learn how to take a loss, fight back, control your emotions, and show up tomorrow. OR you disappear like the rest. 

there is no such thing as a "perfect trader." you must survive long enough to compound. most don't and the ones that do are few and far between.
result.title: no one is coming.

result.message:
can you walk away from a losing trade?
can you walk away from a red day?
can you walk away without forcing a trade?

In this game, this is real test. it’s not about whether you can hit a payout target or catch a massive move. But can you stay alive? can you stay alive when your edge isn’t there? can you sit on your hands all day?

the market is good at luring you in. it’s meant to tempt you and incite/tap in to your emotions. 

how many times have you fomo’d in trying to catch the momentum or chase a move? Then, get smoked in drawdown and forced to close. 

The best traders I know are the ones who don’t do this. they are fine with not trading when nothing is there. they  don't have insane frequency each day. their goal is survive to trade tomorrow. they have a "survival system". 
 
and just because you got a payout doesn’t mean you “won” or a "good risk manager". 

“Winning” in props is taking multiple payouts and scaling your accounts/payouts. Getting to that live account and taking uncapped payouts. That’s winning. 

I think back to the post I made about 'the life of a turkey'. **go back and read it again if you forget. it's relevant here. 

one day can wipe you out. set you back weeks/months of progress. tilt and lack of focus are the biggest culprits. it can happen pretty easily. 

-accidentally leave an order active by mistake and you get wiped out. 
-revenge trade on a losing day to your MLL. 
-dca your entry to infinity and get wicked out at the low/high. 

i've seen it all. also, actually been thru it all. 

be a good risk manager. consider yourself a "risk allocator". that’s all that matters.
result.title: in the shoes of a "good" risk manager.

result.message: the greatest thing you can be called in trading is a “good risk manager”.

result.message:
this is a portfolio management principle i learned studying/reading up on George Soros. if you look how Soros invested, he would be very tight at the beginning of the year. i'm going to use some arbitrary numbers here to demonstrate. 

he would try to get +7% returns on the year. he would "crawl" up to 7%. very tight sizing and little leverage.

once he was up 7%+, he would increase his position sizing and leverage he would use. he would go to "walk" mode. "walk" up to +15%.

once he was up 20%, he would use a ton of leverage and big position sizes. large asymmetric bets. in Soros case, betting a big chunk of AUM betting against the pound. "go time" mentality. 

if he was in run mode and he started losing money, say going from +20% to +15%, he would bring it back to "walk mode". 

a lot of rookies/beginners when they start in "run" mode, they aren't able to "downshift." they hit a drawdown and then start cycling and spiraling to the downside. take it easy. 

as a beginner, if you have never ran a business before, never ran a portfolio before, never managed anything before, take it easy, find your "sea legs" first. feel it out. then, swing.
result.title: crawl walk run.

i hate to be the guy that says DON'T try to make trading work. or DON'T follow your dreams. 

the people that "make it" in trading (and in prop firms) is very, very small. you are against the system. it's an incredibly slim number. you face "restaurant level odds" of failure. i'm not trying to say don't try. you got to realize it's tough. go back to the drawing board and really think about it. it's always tougher than you ever think it is. 

truly "making it" is akin to your odds of making it to the NBA or MLB. 

-how many traders can pass an evaluation? about 47%.

-how many traders can take a payout? about 28%. 

-how many traders can make take multiple payouts and a "livable" wage? a lot less than 30%. 

understand this: it's gonna take a lot of time, money, and energy before even getting to the threshold to begin to start making money. and even then, you aren't guaranteed anything. 

it's unfortunate to say it, but traders making money from trading almost functions like propaganda. i can think of  10 traders in the futures prop space, that make "pretty good" money. it starts to drop off hard after those 10. and even those 10 are probably considered "smaller fishes" in the grand scheme of things.
result.title: the real odds.

very little is free in life. and the things that are free, aren't valued.

result.message:
also, this is equally important. gonna continue with the sports betting analogy. you start off playing $5 or $10 promos. you are just getting your feet wet and learning how things work. in a ‘futures trading’ context, this is micros. for beginners it might be hard to bet small. you come into trading (or sports betting) at the appeal of big profits. thats the appeal for many. but understand those profits come from time and experience. leave the ego at the door. 
 
then, once you start winning and you have to scale and adjust your bet size. (from more micros up to minis). the smaller units and promos become not worth your time. if you have 100k equity, it’s not worth your time to bet $50 or $100 (or a single micro).

you size up to betting $25 units. then, $50. it keeps scaling upward. it makes sense to logically keep scaling this bet size until it’s having market impact. in sports betting, that’s moving the line. in trading, that’s market impact and execution impact. in live markets, it’s harder to fill more contracts (not a shocker). in simulated environments, size is not a factor (in a market impact sense). that’s the advantage to sim environments. in live, there must be a buyer and seller for each fill. for example, it’s hard to fill 50 or 100 mini contracts outside market hours. your orders will get front ran. to guarantee execution, you might have to execute at market which is an execution cost. (fee differential market vs limit)

there’s this whole math formula for calculating optimal bet size. called kelly criterion. not very relevant for futures trading albeit. i encourage you all to look into and read up about it. in betting, you can find ev by devigging advertised line. from there, there’s different adaptations and philosophies on implementations and adaptions. quarter kelly? half kelly? i don’t want to get too deep into this. *it’s also the same thought as poker. (maybe 
@SellingRips
 might have a comment on this?)

the idea here is scaling based off account and equity value. you have to know when it’s time to increase your “units size” and bet more.
result.title: when it's time to size up.

result.message:
the biggest thing that plagues prop traders is consistency. and for good reason. it’s by design. it’s hard to be consistent. i have this theory that everyone knows what the right move for them is. everyone knows what to do. but people do not want to do it. 

think about health and fitness. you know you should eat healthier. you know you need to go to the gym and workout. you know to get more sleep and drink water. but can you actually do it? and not do it once, but do it consistently. but still, you search for cheat codes and fast solutions. it’s also the reason why glp-1 and ozempic have become so popular recently.

you know exactly what’s RIGHT for you. but you still want to be told what to do. you know exactly what’s WRONG and what you shouldn’t be doing. BUT, you will keep repeating it. over and over. this is a pattern that you have to break.

it’s gonna require some ego death to break the spell. it’s easy to identify the symptoms and pinpoint where you went wrong. the hard part is identifying the causes. 

can you walk away on a green day? can you not revenge trade after a few red trades? traders demand for platforms to do this for you. they want the platform to lock you out. platforms shouldn’t have to stop you. you should. the most dangerous trait in a funded trader: discipline for 5 days, tilt on day 6. stop demanding simplicity and resisting behavior change.

i can give all the best tips and pointers i got. you can search for all the answers to the questions you have on the internet. but when nothing has to change, most things just… don’t.
result.title: on consistency. (again)

result.message:
For new traders entering the space, your options are:

a. save up $25,000 to fund a personal live account
b. buy an "evaluation" from a prop firm ($100)

Option A allows you to call yourself a "real trader". I have seen hundreds of people go this route and lose money they can not afford to lose paying "tuition" to learn the game of trading. This cycle is arguably worse as loans, kid's college funds, and months of salaries are wiped away.

Option B allows you to pay a much smaller fixed cost for the same education. it enables you to play around in a controlled environment teaching you risk, sizing, emotions, and learning how to navigate events. prop firms are the cheapest way to buy experience in trading. additionally, these prop firms allow you to lever up when  you have the experience to do so. your earning potential scales with your fixed costs. this creates a much tighter and defined risk profile (higher sharpe).

Yes, 90% of sim traders fail. But 90% of live traders do too. at least with props, the graveyard cost is much smaller.
result.title: props are power.

result.message:
i like to view this thru the lens of sports betting. if you have ever bet before, they use this term that I really love:  Units

it’s a dynamic system and a way to size risk relative to your conviction.

on a random baseball game, maybe you risk 0.5 units. 

a playoff game where you see value and think you have an edge? maybe 3-5 units. the size is dynamic. it changes with your conviction and judgment.

traders (for some reason) don’t think this way. they usually trade the same size and the same setup with the same fixed risk. you should learn that not every trade demands the same exposure. (knowing when to bet .5 units or 5 units)
 
act as a risk allocator (I’ve said this before). focus on taking risk adjusted bets. don't think about it like your job is to just "find trades or setups." it’s to allocate your risk and utilize the best products to do so. if you do not know your unit size, you are really just gambling, not trading. 

learn to size your bets. it will unlock scaling.
result.title: sizing your bets.

result.message:
one reason why you are “stuck” in your trading journey is that you are not testing yourself enough. 
 
i’ve talked to a lot of traders and they all put labels on themselves. 
 
“I’m a breakout trader.” 
“I’m an ICT trader.”
“I’m a [insert whatever label here] trader”
 
most traders find early success with one strategy. but then it stops there. they will learn one way to win and make it their whole identity. when it stop working, they panic. they try to keep optimizing that one strategy. 
 
here’s what i do instead: i use a 70/20/10 formula. 
 
what do i mean by that? 
 
– 70% = repeat what’s proven
– 20% = study new tools + concepts
– 10% = actually try them live

70% of the time, i’m doing what i know works for me and has proven to work for me. my core edge and typical setups i take. 
 
20% of my time is spent learning new things. 
learn footprints. learn TPOs. learn volume analysis. learn bet sizing. learn liquidity. learn psychology. learn timing. 
 
10% is pure experimentation. you got to spend time actually trying these new things. 
try different markets. try crypto and forex. try options. try anything out of comfort zone. it probably won’t work for you. you have to be comfortable doing things that might not work. i would’ve never discovered prop trading if i kept telling myself i was a crypto guy. i would have missed out on what you see now. 
 
you will never know if you do not test it. you don’t grow by repeating what’s familiar. 
 
you will plateau if you stop testing new things. your “label” and your “identity” makes you feel safe. in trading, comfort kills. you will only grow by dropping the label. 
 
markets are always evolving. nothing will work forever. your job is to adapt. 
 
the best traders i know are part scientists. they don’t stop testing new things and new ideas they have. they expand their perspective. the traders who don’t test new ideas eventually become traders who can’t adapt to different market conditions and opportunities. 

BE BRAVE ENOUGH TO DROP THE LABEL.
result.title: the 70/20/10 method.

result.message: I FUCKING HATE LABELS.
result.title: the death of identity trading.

result.message:
a turkey lives for 1000 days. then one day, it all comes to an end. a turkey doesn't know when his last day will be. most prop traders are the same way. 

you can stack a few green days in a row. maybe even a few weeks. your confidence grows. the green days feed your confidence. your guard drops. then, one day you take a trade and it starts going against you. you add more contracts. you size up. then, you start praying it reverses. 

you start getting away from your rules. maybe you even shrug off your daily loss limit. the trade keeps going against you. then, its all over. your account is gone. all your progress wiped out in one day. 

now, you have to start back over again. and the whole cycle repeats. the past success you built makes you feel safe, but remember the market doesn't care about your feelings. like the turkey, you don’t see the end coming. all you see is the farmer’s hand, and it’s fed you every day. until it doesn't. it's a game of survival. 

i call it the "TURKEY TRADER FALLACY".

I get asked this a lot. How do you scale? What does scaling mean?

you gotta build a “buffer” first. sure KJ, what does that mean? you gotta start winning first. you guys might think this is just about trading but it’s applicable to life as well. trading wise, it's a few green days/weeks. don’t try to run before you walk. or it's guaranteed failure. this is hard and most can’t do this. not everyone can be winners (dark right?). the odds are always against you.

now that you won a little bit, what can you do to get to take it to the next level? this is even harder. there’s plenty of one hit wonders out there. these people are the ones who hit the big home run. you are gonna need some luck and some good variance to get here. you need survivability. that’s what the buffer is. 

It’s protection. It buys time, buys reps, buys you another shot. weaponize these early wins into something bigger. you might feel successful already. but you have to have an open mind about bigger. compound everything. 

you’re gonna fail even more here, but allow your bet size to remain in balance so you can take multiple shots. don’t put all your eggs in one basket. whether you are starting a business or you just got your funded account, it’s all the same idea here. utilize the “buffer” here. take risk adjusted bets.

GOODLUCK, YOU ARE GONNA NEED IT.

result.message:
I got asked to make a post about tilting. I guess we can call it “TILT THURSDAY.”
 
there was a time where i didn’t call it tilt. i just called it “making my money back.” but really, it was my anxiety coming out. it was fear disguised as urgency. (I’ve said this before, but I really mean it.)

i’d lose a trade, and my emotions would change. i’d start rushing. overthinking. pressing buttons for the sake of feeling in control again. and once it starts, nothing can save you. not your edge, not even the setup on the chart.

The moment you start trading emotionally, you’ve already lost. people think tilt is reversing positions, revenge trading. blowing accounts. but tilt is quieter to me. It’s before it really falls apart. 

TO ME, TILT IS 3 THINGS:
-tilt is telling yourself you’ll size down… but don’t.
-tilt is breaking your rules “just this once.”
-tilt is holding a loser a little longer, because “it’s gotta bounce.”

the hard part is that it doesn’t feel like tilt when it’s happening. it feels like a prayer. you call it intuition. and that’s how it gets you.

what helped me break out of it wasn’t "locking in". i don't like that phrase. it was self awareness. awareness that tilt doesn’t start with the trade. it starts the second your identity gets involved in the trade.

you think:
“i should win this trade.”
“i have to end green.”
“i need to fix this.”

so how do you stop it? you stop tying your worth to the end result. zoom out. get flat before it takes over. take a pause. 
 
Remind yourself: the goal isn’t to win today. the goal is to be able to trade and scale tomorrow. and the next day. and the day after that.

YOU DON’T WIN BACK YOUR “EDGE.” YOU GOTTA PROTECT IT.
result.title: tilting starts in your head.

result.message:
back in december, i had a day where i knew better and still reset by the end of the day. i woke up a little off. didn’t feel sharp. but i thought maybe i could trade my way into rhythm. took a setup i liked, early near open. it didn’t work. i sized up on the next one. didn’t wait. didn’t think. just clicked.

within minutes, 5 accounts were gone. it stung.

and for a second, i tried to blame the volatility. the market. literally anything besides myself for an hour.

but deep down, i knew. it wasn’t the setup or market. it was me.

what makes you consistent isn’t how you handle green days. it’s how you respond when the trade doesn’t work how you thought it would. It’s when the plan gets uncomfortable. Its when the tilt creeps in quietly like it did to me that day.

after the reset, i didn’t touch a chart for 2 days. i wasn’t mad. i had gotten over it, but i had to face what happened. really sit with it. break down what part of me still needed to win right now.

i was in the reset trap because i refused to slow down. since then, my performance YTD hasn’t come home runs. Instead, it’s coming from the singles, doubles, and the triples. sorry I played baseball as a kid, so I had to use that one. But seriously, think smaller.

that day in december? i lost money, but i found something way more important in myself that would prepare me for everything next.
result.title: the reset that revealed everything.

result.message:
HERE’S WHAT I THOUGHT:
all i need was the perfect setup.
the right entry. then size up.

scale fast.
hit the leaderboard.
never look back.

What I missed was not prioritizing survival first.

You need a “survival system.”

or else you are building a house on a cliff.

think about a new kitchen.
marble countertops. open floor plans. picking the perfect “look”.
the dream payout. the flashy strategy. the special “model”.
don’t overthink it. it's the same idea.

the “look” and appearance attracts more eyes than the foundation. few ever think to ask about it.

then the wind comes one day.
you hit a drawdown.
you tilt. break rules and reset.
and the whole house collapses.

its the same idea as pouring concrete before hanging drywall.

-your risk floor (your ability to stay alive) comes first.

-pay attention to drawdown.

-dynamically adjust your position sizing.

-watch for news.

-control your emotions.

the boring stuff.
the stuff that actually keeps you in the game.

edge means nothing if you blow up before it plays out.

i learned this the hard way.
built “progress” on top of shaky risk habits.
got paid once or twice. then reset again and again.
chased strategy after strategy while the cracks kept appearing and widening.

I stopped trying to run before I could walk.

I only thought about survival.
then, things finally stabilized.
stability comes before scale.

foundations aren't laid overnight.
only once i knew i could survive a losing day. could i aim for a winning month.

so if you’re wondering what to focus on, maybe don’t ask what your best indicator or setup is.

look at your house. can it survive a storm? a storm will come at some point. Its inevitable.

and when it does, only the houses with strong foundations stay standing.

-KJ🖤

*hope the metaphor was good
result.title: a “survival system.”

result.message:
not sure if this is intentionally ignorant or just a misinformed opinion…

prop trading isn’t the same as trading. this shouldn’t come as a surprise. it’s the same reason behind why top prop traders cycle accounts. most of them aren’t bad traders. they just understand the model and the economics of what’s at play. 

let me break this down. imagine you are a dog and every morning you fetch a ball and you receive a treat. over time, those treats increase exponentially. by the end of the first week, you are receiving 20+ treats per day. then, one day it all changes. your owner changes the rules of the game. now, you have to fetch 10 balls to receive a single treat. you realize that fetching 10 balls doesn’t carry the same value as before. you stop fetching and the owner is perplexed! 

why is this happening the owner asks? why is my dog no longer fetching the balls? the owner runs a cross sectional study at this point. he can’t figure out why you are no longer chasing the ball! the owner starts research the day after the change. the cross sectional data confuses him even more! he can’t figure out what’s happening!

the dog was never wrong! the dog just understood something you didn’t!

the best understand expected value. they understand incentives. the crowd is kept intentionally ignorant and blind. it’s by design. as i’m sure you are aware of.  don’t think in black and white. understand the game the dog is playing.
result.title: understand the game.

result.message:
here’s why this matters.
every trade is a surface-level bet wrapped in invisible assumptions. in trading. and in life. 

you think you’re betting on direction but you’re also betting on:
• platform integrity
• execution latency
• slippage
• payout reliability
• time constraint
• model validity

example:
you nail the direction, but get wicked out because a news bot front-ran a fake headline.
or you pass a prop challenge, then realize they changed the withdrawal rules. 

they don't appear obvious to you and most are blind. but they are unpriced bets and they could bankrupt you. 

if you are serious and approach trading as a business, this has to keep you up at night. 

the tool matters just as much as the trader using it.
result.title: hidden bets everywhere.

until firms incentivize trading live capital, this space isn’t moving forward.
handing traders a $10K sim account and offering either a small live account or a payout doesn’t create long-term alignment.
there has to be bonuses, clear paths, and real upside for staying past the simulated phase.

result.message:
Most traders are able to pass a few evaluations.

But the real evaluation comes after.

Prop firms are designed to stress-test your behavior under pressure:

PNL constraints.  No forgiveness.
Time limits. Daily caps.
Invisible traps everywhere.

It’s not enough to be skilled, you have to adapt in real time to optimize for payouts. 

I call it the “Failure Funnel”. The Failure Funnel punishes confidence without structure.

If you don’t come in with a system built for the rules,
you’ll fail the same way everyone else does.

Fast.
result.title: the failure funnel.

result.message:
My historical evaluation pass rate is 40%.

But that didn’t matter until I learned how to scale after.

If you’re still struggling to pass, this probably isn’t for you.

Because if you can’t adapt under evaluation rules, you won’t survive in the funded stage. You have to crawl before you can run. 

Scaling starts where most people stop.

result.message:
I didn’t start with prop firms.

I started with stocks back in 2019. Months later, penny stocks and Options. Got into crypto in early 2020. My first big winer was Tesla inclusion, then Solana in late 2021, and then Hyperliquid. I was always chasing the hottest thing. But I wasn’t always structured.

When I started trading futures, I treated it like a side experiment. I didn’t have much credit (95% of NW in crypto). At the time, the XFA model felt like a test run. I wasn’t fully locked in. I lost a few accounts. Small losses on paper, but the emotional damage was heavier. Losing 20k after my first payout hit hard.

Props required something different, not better setups, but a system built specifically for their rules. I couldn’t just bring over what worked in crypto. I had to engineer a new model, my own proprietary prop system, one that accounted for drawdown limits, time factors, resets, and scaling. 

It forced a decision: stay casual or go all in.

So I went all in.

I rebuilt everything. Started treating every firm like an investment. Used the rules as guidelines. Designed trade plans around daily drawdown math. And from that point on, things changed. Not overnight, but with momentum.

Futures gave me focus. Props gave me constraints.
But my system? That’s what gave me scale.
result.title: my journey with prop firms.

result.message:
The biggest realization?

That the strategy isn’t the whole system. You can have the perfect setup and still lose... if your structure can’t hold the weight.

I learned this the hard way.

There was a stretch, when I first started trading props last year, where I was technically right…
but my system wasn't build to hold it. Payouts would come, then go. I’d reset. Try to get back up. But I wasn’t scaling. Just treading water.

The turning point wasn’t a better trade.
It was building the framework around the trade.
A system of rules. Drawdown usage. Time blocks. Firm awareness. Energy management.

I stopped trying to force success through setups
and started engineering conditions for consistency.
result.title: system > setup

result.message:
Writing’s been feeling more natural lately. I like when it’s a conversation: questions in, thoughts out. Less noise, more signal.

Unfortunately, this space has failed you. Your attention is the product. And most creators treat it that way. They optimize for reach, not connection. They speak to audiences, not with people. 

I don’t want to go down this path.
I’m not here to perform. I’m here to bring back dialogue. If I’m going to say something, it should matter.
If you’re going to listen, it should mean something.

result.message:
yeah. in more ways than one.
chasing this path costs you things most people don’t see. not just time or sleep, but closeness. trust. moments you never get back.

when you start trading, you’re choosing a life most people don’t get. and that means sometimes, they don’t get you anymore.

not everyone makes the journey with you.
but that doesn’t mean they were never part of the story. we all carry something we’ve lost. but some of us also build something with it.

my relationship ended in December.
I was out of town when it happened.
blindsided. alone.

it wasn’t just the loss. it was what it surfaced.
that quiet, familiar thought: maybe I wasn’t enough. maybe I never would be.

but this time… I did something different. I stopped putting myself last. I stopped waiting for the world to see me.

and I got to work. in the gym. on my mind. and on the charts. every ounce of pain became progress. not out of spite, but out of necessity.

maybe I needed it. not the breakup, but the moment that forced me to choose myself.

we don’t get to control the moment. but we do get to choose what we do next.

and that’s where everything changed.
result.title: what i lost.

result.message:
Here’s the truth:
Nobody wants to learn anymore.

I thought I’d start by posting some educational content here and try to help people out.
Break down what worked for me. Walk through a few of my trades. The problem is I’m not a content creator. I’m a trader. I didn’t start this to build a platform. I started posting because I kept seeing people doing it wrong.

The state of the space is that theatrics outperform substance. And the algorithm rewards the illusion of progress over actual growth.

So I’m not going to try to “create content.”
I realized if I wanted people to actually learn from me…
I have to build something people would take seriously.
Something they couldn’t just scroll past and forget.

And I needed a way to actually show people what I learned.
Not for engagement.
Not for likes.
But for transparency.

That’s where all of this started.
More coming.
result.title: theatrics outperforming substance.

result.message:
Here are my honest thoughts on the prop industry lately.

Recycled content. Repeated jargon. Copy and paste strategies. Straight gambling. Everyone’s got an affiliate code.

I’ve been watching it quietly for a while now.

People don’t trade for edge anymore. They trade for engagement. They don’t pass challenges to build consistency. They gamble to screenshot the dashboard, farm likes, and sell a $99 Discord. 

If you trade ICT? You’re wrong.
If you don’t trade ICT? You’re also wrong.

Twitter/X turned into a war of chasing numbers and egos, and honestly… I wanted no part of it.

So I stayed quiet.

No courses.
No Discords.
No referrals.
No “DM me bro.”

Just putting in the work every day. Only trading, no BS. 

I passed evaluations. I took payouts. I refined my system until it almost ran itself. And while everyone else was chasing the next news event to gamble on, I built a process to take a million in payouts in 2025. 

But here’s the thing:

That silence wasn’t by accident.
It was the edge. 

There comes a point where silence stops being an edge. You learn enough that sharing becomes the next evolution.

Gonna be more active on here. 

-KJ
result.title: silence=power. sometimes.
